title: SEC Commissioner Greg Sankey says talk of leaving the NCAA is "real"
description: As college football tries to navigate an era of chaos sparked by the reckoning from decades of antitrust violations, one of the most powerful conference claims it's serious about leaving the NCAA.
fullText:
As college football tries to navigate an era of chaos sparked by the reckoning from decades of antitrust violations, one of the most powerful conference claims it's serious about leaving the NCAA.

Via Nick Schultz of On3.com, SEC Commissioner Greg Sankey said Monday that his conference could indeed break away and do its own thing.

“You phrased the question, ‘How real?’ So I’ll take that as, like, real or not. So they’re real,” Sankey said. “People have talked about that. They’ve opined about the frustrations that bring them to the point of saying we should look at something significantly different."

Sankey claims he's not simply trying to secure an edge for the SEC in ongoing discussions regarding the future of college football.

“I do not believe that is a leverage point — I think that’s just honest communication," Sankey said. "I would prefer that stay in a meeting room or on a video conference and not be part of the D1 ticker the next morning, but I don’t control all of that.”

Leverage or not (and no leverage is worth a damn if the party exercising admits it's leverage), something has to give. And if the proposed solution harms the biggest conferences, why should they go along with it?

While recent years have seen a revolution in college football, the next phase could be an evolution in which major conferences run their own leagues, perhaps with (in time) a postseason tournament consisting of the best teams in each conference.

With the NCAA losing much of its power, the conferences can fill the void. With so many college football teams, maybe the best solution is to have several different leagues that run their own businesses. The various conferences can then do their own thing. Once the conferences are sorted out, the postseason arrangements will follow.

title: How young is the average age of Texas football’s roster?
description: One of the points raised about Texas football’s roster throughout the current offseason has been the average age of the program’s roster heading into the 2026 college football season. In fact, as noted by multiple outlets, Texas has the fourth-youngest roster across the entire sport at an average roster age of 19.86 years old. In […]
fullText:
One of the points raised about Texas football’s roster throughout the current offseason has been the average age of the program’s roster heading into the 2026 college football season. In fact, as noted by multiple outlets, Texas has the fourth-youngest roster across the entire sport at an average roster age of 19.86 years old. In other words, roughly the age of a second-year sophomore.

Most of the discussion around average roster age goes back to a recent RotoWire report.

Here’s an excerpt from how RotoWire broke it down at the time:

“Across all 138 FBS programs, the average roster checks in at just over 20.3 years old — but the gap between the oldest and youngest teams is wider than a full calendar year, and it says a great deal about how each program is built. At the top sits UTSA, whose roster averages 21.02 years old — the oldest in the country. At the other end … the youngest roster in the sport at 19.81. Neither is an accident. In the transfer-portal era, veteran-heavy rosters and blue-chip youth movements are two different blueprints for winning — and the numbers below show exactly who is leaning which way.”

Clearly, it’s not even a big deal by most measures of the game.

At least those that matter most—wins and losses.

Georgia football, for example, has the game’s current youngest average age roster.

Georgia has also played in consecutive College Football Playoff quarterfinals.

title: Which Oregon Ducks cracked the college fantasy football top-300 rankings?
description: The Oregon Ducks can help you win your college fantasy football league, with nine players listed in the top-300 rankings.
fullText:
College football fans are entering uncharted territory this season, with college fantasy football coming to the public, thanks to Yahoo Sports. Just like people have done for decades in pro sports, fans can build a fantasy roster with some of the nation's top talents, now at the collegiate level.

If you're trying to win your league, it's probably a good idea to examine different rosters to find quality talent and sleepers to draft. However, if you're only looking to draft the Oregon Ducks in your league, look no further.

Oregon is expected to have one of the most explosive offenses in the country this season and one of the most seasoned defensive units. Drafting only Ducks could minimize your total point value in matchups, especially if Dan Lanning pulls the starters early in a blowout, but make no mistake, some Ducks will go early in your drafts.

Yahoo Sports revealed a top-300 ranking for players in 2026 drafts, and nine Ducks cracked the list, including the team offense and defense. Let's take a look at where the Ducks rank and when you should look to scoop them up in your draft.

Hill is coming off an explosive freshman season where he racked up 793 total yards and six touchdowns, and he is only expected to improve upon those numbers as a sophomore. With Noah Whittington no longer blocking his path, Hill will be Oregon's go-to receiving back, and if he improves in pass protection, he could fight for more snaps. Hill is lower on the list of running backs because of the split backfield with Davison, but his upside as a receiver and explosive runs off the edges make him an incredible pick.

Davison scored 15 touchdowns as a freshman and was Oregon's favorite back in short-yardage situations, rarely failing to move the chains. Davison can operate as the starter in a split backfield, getting opportunities to help drive the field. However, even if he is off the field for a drive, as soon as the Ducks enter goal-to-go territory, you can expect him to carry the rock. Looking for easy touchdowns? Davison is your guy.

Moore is a bit lower in the wideout rankings as well simply because of the amount of targets the Ducks have to get the ball to. However, Moore is undoubtedly the top receiver in the Oregon offense, thanks to his track speed and ability to win in tight man coverage. Moore can play on the perimeter or out of the slot, using his speed to win over the middle. Moore is a great option as a receiver on your fantasy team, but there could be games where other Ducks eclipse him in production.

Tight end is always a tricky position to draft in fantasy. You can wait and stock up at higher scoring positions, or you can jump early to get one of the best producers at the position. Johnson is arguably the top tight end in the country, and he was already a massive receiving threat as the backup last season, making 32 catches for 510 yards and three touchdowns. Now that he is the starter, his production should only increase.

If you're worried about Stewart needing to work through some rust after missing all of last season with an injury, that's fair, but far from a good reason to avoid drafting him. Stewart looked great in the Oregon spring game, which was his first game action since tearing his ACL last summer. Stewart is Oregon's best vertical threat and capable of winning 50/50 balls. He also has plenty of experience and should be featured heavily in the red zone this season, giving him more opportunities to score.

Quarterback is typically a position you can wait on in your draft, but Moore's rank as the ninth best quarterback in college fantasy football might surprise some. However, rushing statistics reward the quarterback position 

A new wrinkle in college fantasy football is the ability to draft team offenses, and this is where Oregon could really shine. Offenses are scored by the points and yards they accumulate. They can also gain or lose points based on if the team wins or loses, and how many turnovers are committed. Oregon does a great job of taking care of the football, and there is potential to be among the highest scoring teams in the nation this season. In a blowout, the Ducks could take their foot off the gas and leave some points on the board. Either way, it's hard not to see the Ducks putting up big numbers in just about every game on the schedule this season.

McClellan is not a sleeper pick to any Oregon fan, but he might be a sleeper fantasy pick to the rest of the country. He quietly finished last season with 38 catches for 558 yards and three touchdowns, earning more snaps as the season went on. He arguably has the best hands on the team, known for making some ridiculous grabs, and is a pure red zone threat because of that ability alone. He is probably closer to Iverson Hooks than he is to Dakorien Moore in terms of his role within the offense, but there is the potential for some explosive plays downfield.

Defense in college football can come at a premium, and the scoring in fantasy is all about point suppression and turnovers. The Ducks had 15 interceptions, six fumble recoveries and three defensive touchdowns last season, all while allowing 17.9 points per game, 12th-best nationally. Oregon's young secondary has more experience coming into this season, so I'm more inclined to believe there won't be as many explosive plays against the group this season. Also, the defensive line is the best in the country and should wreak havoc in the backfield and rack up sacks all season long.

Contact/Follow @Ducks_Wire on X (formerly Twitter) and like our page on Facebook to follow ongoing coverage of Oregon Ducks news, notes, and opinions.

This article originally appeared on Ducks Wire: Which Oregon Ducks cracked the college fantasy football top-300 rankings?

fullText:
After Yahoo! Sports reported last week the SEC was considering “Plan B” amid talks about the Protect College Sports Act, commissioner Greg Sankey confirmed those talks are not just a negotiating play. He said the discussions are “real” as the college athletics landscape shifts.

“You phrased the question, ‘How real?’ So I’ll take that as, like, real or not. So they’re real,” Sankey said at SEC media days. “People have talked about that. They’ve opined about the frustrations that bring them to the point of saying we should look at something significantly different.

“I do not believe that is a leverage point. I think that’s just honest communication. I would prefer that stay in a meeting room or on a video conference and not be part of the D1.ticker the next morning, but I don’t control all of that.”

title: Where the top QBs in Minnesota are heading for college
description: Minnesota is deep at quarterback this year. Maybe not with Power Four recruits, but there is a good crop who have the ability to play on the next level from the Gophers to NAIA and Division III. Here are some of the candidates. COMMITTED JETT FEENEY – Moorhead | MinnesotaTook the Spuds to the state […]
fullText:
Minnesota is deep at quarterback this year. Maybe not with Power Four recruits, but there is a good crop who have the ability to play on the next level from the Gophers to NAIA and Division III. Here are some of the candidates.

JETT FEENEY – Moorhead | Minnesota
Took the Spuds to the state title game. Athletic and can improvise. Throws with touch when he needs to. Smart with intangibles. Was a great take for the Gophers. Should be in the running for Minnesota’s Mr. Football.

CADEN GUTZMER – Minnetonka | North Dakota
Mobile 6’2 prospect who makes a variety of throws and is comfortable throwing on the move but can also keep the ball on an RPO and hurt defenses with his feet. One of the crown jewels of the UND class.

OSBORNE LOPEZ – Mankato West | Minnesota-Duluth
6’4, 215 pounds and got off to a great start last season before suffering an injury. Should have a big junior year. Only three games as a starter, but big and talented.

PARKER KUEHN – Springfield | Dordt College
Has a bigger frame at 6-foot-3, 180 pounds, and is a three-sport athlete who put put up some good testing numbers this off-season.

KARSEN HERMAN – Waconia | St. Thomas
St. Thomas was happy to keep Herman at home. He’s a multi-sport athlete with size (6-5, 205 pounds) and throws with anticipation. Also shows some mobility and ability to make plays with his feet.

TRISTAN KOWALKOWSKI – Becker
Posted good track and testing times at 6’2, 205 pounds. Sacramento State, Fordham, Central Michigan, Akron and Southern Miss have offered.

THOR SOUKUP – Wayzata
Physically strong 6’2, 220-pound prospect who also plays lacrosse. Northern Arizona and Fordham have offered.

ELI RODEMEYER – Byron
Had a solid junior year with 19 total touchdowns and was rewarded with several offers including Augustana, Sioux Falls and others. Has 4.0 GPA and also participated in basketball and track.

BECKHAM VANVOORST – Robbinsdale Cooper
Good athlete with a live arm at 6’2, 200 pounds. Can run with the ball and make plays with his feet as well as hang in the pocket and make throws. Northern Michigan and Sioux Falls are among his offers.

ETHAN RYAN – Holy Family Catholic
6-foot-3 with a smooth release. Has a handful of offers or roster spot offers from Jamestown, Gustavus and Saint Johns.

SAWYER SENST – Hermantown
Has great size at 6’6, 240 pounds and has a basketball background. Suffered an injury but working back through it. Some schools may want to look at him at other positions too. Rocky Mountain College, an NAIA program, has offered.

title: I skipped college and now earn over $40 an hour in a blue-collar job. The AI boom is creating more opportunities for me.
description: Cody Cole chose to enter the trades after high school rather than going to college. Now, he makes over $40 an hour working on data centers.
fullText:
Cody Cole started working in the electrical field at 18 years old, making $15 an hour.

He now makes over $40 an hour, which has allowed him to be the sole provider for his family.

Cole said the AI boom has helped open up more job opportunities within the industry.

This as-told-to essay is based on a conversation with Cody Cole, 25, an Arizona-based electrical field superintendent with Corbins, an industrial electrical contractor. The following has been edited for length and clarity.

I started in the electrical field in 2019 at 18 years old, shortly after getting out of high school. At first, I wasn't particularly thrilled about doing electrical work. But my brother, dad, uncle, and grandpa all worked with their hands, and I'm very similar to them, so I thought I'd give it a try.

As I immersed myself more and saw all the positive career opportunities that arose, I found that I began to like my role.

Now, seven years later, I'm a superintendent making over $40 an hour with 45 people who report to me. It's helped relieve the financial stress my wife and I have from everyday life, as we raise three kids and give them the best upbringing we can.

I've never been amazing at school; it wasn't something I looked forward to. My parents taught me to pay my own way and earn my own money — something I greatly appreciate — so paying for a traditional college education wasn't something I wanted to do.

With a career in industrial electrical work, I knew I was able to start making good money right out of high school.

The AI boom also changed my perspective on my career in electrical. I've seen firsthand that there's going to continue to be a need for skilled tradesmen; at my company, Corbins, we specialize in data centers, which are going to be needed with the expansion of AI.

After entering the industry without any formal training, one of the turning points of my career came a year and a half in, when I had the opportunity to do an electrical apprenticeship program through the Western Electrical Contractors Association (WECA).

Corbins would fund my schooling and pay my salary while I attended classes for two-week blocks every three to six months, across four years. The program consisted of a minimum of 156 hours of classroom instruction a year and 8,000 hours of on-the-job training.

I couldn't justify not going to school — it was such a great opportunity. I ended up graduating as salutatorian in 2024.

I had started out making $15 an hour — which was awesome for not having any experience — but I've progressed quickly from doing hands-on labor to being a foreman to being a superintendent. Now my hourly rate is in the high $40s.

I could've even started making more, faster, if I'd jumped into the apprenticeship sooner.

I don't feel I'd be in the financial position to be the sole provider of my family, had I chosen a different path. We've been fortunate that with my income, we've been able to achieve our goal of my wife being a full-time stay-at-home mom.

There's so much opportunity for young people. One of the foremen who works with me is only 21, and he's phenomenal at his job. Another superintendent I work with is also amazing, and he's only 23 or 24.

There's a lot of job security in what we do. With the expansion of AI going at an extreme rate, there's plenty of work all over the country with these jobs. The opportunities are endless — not only on the construction side of things, with physical, hands-on labor, but also with the other positions it takes to build these data centers, such as accounting, financing, and planning.

For the last two years, I've been working fully on data center projects. I've been fortunate enough to travel to New Mexico a few times and to Texas; the data centers are going up all over the place, so the work is there.

I know there are controversies around data centers. I'm not an expert, but I believe the ones I'm working on are quiet and not intrusive. They're also very necessary for the way technology is going, in order for AI to progress, and are providing new work opportunities, like the ones I've had.

Although I'm now a superintendent, I still feel like I'm new to the trades and there's still a ton that I want to learn. However, I'm enjoying being able to mentor others and see the bigger picture of the projects we're working on.

When I was learning, people taught me the "why" behind what I was doing; that's something I'm now able to share with the people I work with, especially as the AI industry and data centers are fairly new.

In my role, I plan for the whole building, but in the higher positions, they're looking at an even larger picture of the impact and what's coming next. Understanding the big picture of projects is an aspect of my career I really enjoy and am looking forward to continuing.

I don't want to do something just because I'm told to — I want to know why I'm doing it. It makes the work more meaningful, and makes me want to try harder, give the job my full effort, and train those around me so they can learn from me too.

For the people I manage, and for people who are new to the industry, data center construction gives them an opportunity to get into the industry as it's booming, learn, and progress their careers quickly.

Do you have a story to share about pursuing a blue-collar career rather than a corporate one? Contact this editor, Jane Zhang, at janezhang@businessinsider.com.

If you enjoyed this story, be sure to follow Business Insider on Yahoo.

title: Dan Lanning sets the standard Lincoln Riley needs to match at USC
description: Dan Lanning has positioned the Oregon Ducks where they need to be in the college football world. Playoff wins are what Lincoln Riley lacks
fullText:
The Oregon Ducks, since coming into the Big Ten, have raised their standard of play in the conference. Dan Lanning has put the Ducks in the College Football Playoff the past two seasons. With the continuity and talent the Ducks have on both sides of the ball, this should be their year. Lincoln Riley and USC want it to be the Trojans' year instead.

In a recent article, Zachary Neel breaks down how Dan Lanning is viewed among top coaches in the country:

"According to Wasserman, Lanning is the No. 3 head coach in the conference, behind defending national champion Curt Cignetti and Indiana, and former defending national champion Ryan Day at Ohio State.

"After being blown out in the College Football Playoff two consecutive years, Oregon and Lanning are the brunt of many jokes," Wasserman writes. "People view the Ducks as a very good team, but not one that will ever be good enough to break through in the CFP. That’s wrong. And dangerous. Though Oregon has not been the best team in the country at any point during Lanning’s leadership, it has steadily been a force in talent acquisition and retention. Lanning is 48-8 overall, and his teams will consistently make the CFP. Oregon may not have won a national title under his leadership, but it seems only a matter of time before it breaks through. Heck, it could be in 2026."

Dan Lanning has consistently kept his teams in the conversation. Unfortunately, he hasn't been able to lead his teams past his counterparts. Ryan Day and Curt Cignetti have the consistency and the championship. That is what Dan Lanning is hoping to achieve this season. Lincoln Riley is currently just chasing what Dan Lanning has been able to cultivate in the Big Ten. Riley, this season, for sure needs to put USC in the conversation.

This article originally appeared on Trojans Wire: Dan Lanning sets the standard Lincoln Riley needs to match at USC

title: Golf: High school state champ Tian wins Ohio Women's Amateur
description: Meadow Tian added to her impressive list of pre-college accomplishments Friday, July 17, by outlasting the field to win the 103rd Women’s Ohio Amateur Championship at Moraine Country Club. The victory earned Tian a spot in the U.S. Women’s Amateur at The Honors Course in Ooltewah, Tennessee, on August 4-9. Tian, who will be a freshman at Illinois this fall, began the week with rounds of 72 and ...
fullText:
Meadow Tian added to her impressive list of pre-college accomplishments Friday, July 17, by outlasting the field to win the 103rd Women’s Ohio Amateur Championship at Moraine Country Club.

The victory earned Tian a spot in the U.S. Women’s Amateur at The Honors Course in Ooltewah, Tennessee, on August 4-9.

Tian, who will be a freshman at Illinois this fall, began the week with rounds of 72 and 70 to place second in the qualifying rounds and enter the 32-player match play tournament as the No. 2 seed. She completed her quest with a 3 and 2 victory over Bowling Green’s Addison Kartusch in the 36-hole final.

Tian took the lead on the third hole and led by three twice until Kartusch won holes 13, 14 and 15 to tie the match. Tian regained the lead on 17. On the second 18, Kartusch forged ties on holes 2 and 5. But Tian regained control and led by four twice until both players parred 16. That left Tian up three with two holes to play and ended the match.

Tian, an Olentangy High School graduate, won the 2025 Division I individual state championship. In 2025, Tian also was the Southern Ohio Junior PGA champion, the Central Ohio district player of the year and the Ohio Girls Junior champion. In 2024, she finished second in the state high school tournament.

Tian reached the final with two dominant wins Thursday. In the quarterfinals, she defeated Furman’s Audrey Ryu of Dublin 4 and 2 and Columbus Academy senior Eva Lim 5 and 4.

Kartusch defeated Ohio University player and Centerville graduate Morgan Rodgers in Wednesday's first round of match play. Then she eliminated Sarah Willis, the former Penn State player and two-time state champion from Eaton.

On Thursday, Kartusch defeated Kent State’s Kristen Belden of Canton 3 and 1. Then she defeated Rutgers sophomore Elizabeth Ryu of Dublin 2 and 1 in the semifinals.

Zitzner seventh: Springfield 12-year-old Chaz Zitzner placed seventh at the Junior World Championships at Torrey Pines in San Diego on Thursday.

Zitzner fell off the pace on the first day with a 77 because of a quintuple-bogey 9 on his third hole and a double-bogey 6 three holes later. But he rallied with a

pair of 2-under par 70s to finish the tournament at 1-over par.

Yubo Shen of Thailand won the 11-12 age division at 9-under par with rounds of 72, 67 and 68.

title: JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon says 300,000 workers are needed to rebuild American shipbuilding—with jobs paying $100,000 without a college degree
description: The American Dream now runs through the skilled trades, according to Jamie Dimon—and his bank is investing $24 million to ramp up training in Philadelphia.
fullText:
JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon warned that the U.S. has fallen dangerously behind on shipbuilding, creating a massive shortage of skilled workers—and a lucrative opportunity for young Americans looking for AI-proof, high-paying jobs.

"We need 300,000 electricians, welders, etc. to build ships in the next five or 10 years," Dimon told CNBC on Tuesday, speaking from the Philadelphia Navy Yard.

Unlike many traditional white-collar career paths, skilled trades apprenticeships often pay workers while they train—putting them on a path to six-figure salaries without taking on college debt.

"It fits what we call the American dream: getting kids skills or all workers' skills that they have jobs that could pay 80-, 90-, $100,000 a year after you know a year or two of training. This lifts up America. It helps build the defense industry," Dimon added.

The Philadelphia Navy Yard alone currently employs about 16,000 workers, but Dimon said that workforce could double over the next five years as domestic shipbuilding ramps up.

To help fuel that growth, JPMorgan announced a $24 million investment through loans and philanthropic grants to support a new submarine manufacturing and assembly facility expected to create 450 permanent jobs. The funding will also expand workforce training and apprenticeship programs for "thousands" of prospective welders, electricals, pipefitters, and other skilled trades workers needed at the Navy Yard.

Joining Dimon was Pennsylvania Sen. David McCormick—husband of Meta president Dina Powell McCormick—who argued that while artificial intelligence has fueled fears about the future of white-collar work, demand for skilled trades has never been stronger.

"If you're an experienced welder or electrician, we can't get enough of you," he said. "We have to have [a] workforce that meets this incredible demand."

But Philadelphia embodies the uphill battle the industry faces. The yard was purchased by Hanwha, a South Korean ship manufacturer, in 2024 for $100 million and currently only delivers one to one-and-a-half ships per year. In South Korea, ships are delivered "basically one a week," according to the CEO of Hanwha Philly Shipyard, David Kim. The apprenticeship can also accommodate only about 20 trainees at a time, compared with roughly 400 simultaneously at Hanwha's facilities in South Korea.

Workforce struggles extend well beyond Pennsylvania.

In the Hampton Roads region of Virginia, there is an estimated shortage of 10,000 shipyard workers, which is expected to grow to 40,000 by 2030, reported WHRO.

title: Get to Know Your Orange: #16 QB, Malachi Nelson
description: The former No. 1 recruit has found a fourth college home in Syracuse
fullText:
It’s time to start preparing for the 2026 Syracuse Orange football season. We’re going through the roster to take a look at each Syracuse player as we get to know a lot of new faces for Year 3 of the Fran Brown Era.

High School/Previous College: Los Aloamitos/USC/Boise State/UTEP

2025 stats: Nelson started the first six games of the season for UTEP. He passed for 1,163 yards, eight touchdowns and nine interceptions last season.

2026 projections: Nelson has a long road ahead of him. Steve Angeli is most likely going to be the starting quarterback when the season starts. Nelson is most likely battling Amari Odom for the primary backup spot. However, the talent that made Nelson a highly touted high school prospect can’t have just completely disappeared. There’s a good bet that Syracuse and Nelson believe they can, together, rediscover that talent. If he does, look out.

How’d he get here?: Nelson committed to Syracuse from UTEP  on January 16 of this year.

What’d recruiting sites say?: Nelson was rated a three-star transfer portal player. Out of high school, he was a consensus five-star recruit. ESPN ranked Nelson the No. 1 overall recruit in the class of 2023.

Interesting nugget o’interest: Listing all of Nelson’s impressive accolades from high school would take some time. But not many people get named their state’s Gatorade Player of the Year, let alone twice, which is what Nelson achieved.

title: Davis, who embraced bullpen role at MSU, embarks on pro career
description: Many a starting pitcher has been resistant to the idea of moving to the bullpen. Ben Davis is not one of those pitchers. He was a starter at Booneville High School. At Itawamba Community College, he both started and relieved. Then at Mississippi State, Davis found his niche as a versatile relief pitcher, able to put out fires at any point in a game.
fullText:
Many a starting pitcher has been resistant to the idea of moving to the bullpen. Ben Davis is not one of those pitchers.

He was a starter at Booneville High School. At Itawamba Community College, he both started and relieved. Then at Mississippi State, Davis found his niche as a versatile relief pitcher, able to put out fires at any point in a game. He came to love that role.

“It’s always fun to be able to come out of the pen, big game on the line, and really being able to put an end to whatever was happening, like if it was a big inning or something like that,” Davis said. “… I’m kind of an adrenaline junkie. I’m not really big on emotion, but being on the mound kind of brings it out. That was my thing, coming out of the pen and doing my own thing.”

He’ll get to keep doing his thing at the professional level. Davis was taken by the Colorado Rockies with the first pick of the fourth round in last weekend’s Major League Baseball draft. When he got the call – while surrounded by family – Davis experienced a feeling similar to when he takes the mound.

“My agent called me, and I really wasn’t expecting a whole lot on Day 1,” he said. “But when they called me, I had a big adrenaline dump. Pretty cool. Plus everybody’s sitting there; it was really cool with everybody there.”

Davis leaves Friday for the team’s spring training facility in Phoenix. The Rockies will then decide whether to send him off to play somewhere or keep him there for some hands-on work. Either way, it’s a dream fulfilled.

Davis was a standout at Booneville as both a pitcher and a catcher. He led the Blue Devils to a state championship in 2021. In his two years at ICC, Davis won 10 games and struck out 104 batters over 104 1/3 innings.

“It’s surreal. You grow up, small town, you always dream of getting to play at Mississippi State. Moving on is always the next goal, but just being able to play there and moving on is awesome,” Davis said.

He became a dependable arm at State. Over two seasons, he was 4-4 with a 3.71 ERA, 118 strikeouts and six saves in 104 1/3 innings. Davis made four starts as a junior and then became a full-time reliever this past season, making 26 appearances.

The 6-foot-4, 230-pounder has a fastball that can touch the upper-90s, and he’s also effective with a slider and sweeper.

“He’s a big horse,” Rockies assistant general manager Tommy Tanous told the team’s website. “You watch him and you don’t want to be in the batter’s box against him.”

Davis’ specific role in the professional ranks is yet to be determined, but he’s just fine with continuing his career as a reliever.

“I feel like I’m kind of the guy that can be called on every day if needed,” he said, “and if I’m not needed, that’s whenever I can kind of rest for a couple days and do it again.”

title: Hallie Burns returns to major conference softball
description: Hallie Burns’ college journey has been turbulent, but it’s finally led her back to the Power 4. On July 4, Burns announced her transfer from Southeastern Louisiana to Baylor. Burns was a standout pitcher at Booneville High School and won three straight state titles with the Blue Devils. “The coaching staff was really welcoming coming onto campus for the first time, and I mean they treated my ...
fullText:
Hallie Burns’ college journey has been turbulent, but it’s finally led her back to the Power 4.

On July 4, Burns announced her transfer from Southeastern Louisiana to Baylor. Burns was a standout pitcher at Booneville High School and won three straight state titles with the Blue Devils.

“The coaching staff was really welcoming coming onto campus for the first time, and I mean they treated my family just like their own,” Burns said of Baylor. “That was really reassuring to have someone that generally cared about more than just my athletic ability, but my family and what they had going on in their lives. That was pretty cool.”

Burns signed with Ole Miss after graduating from Booneville in 2023 but redshirted her freshman year and eventually transferred due to injury issues. Those issues initially followed her to Southeastern Louisiana, but she started pitching like her old self this past year.

Burns went 20-5 with a 1.68 ERA to go with 102 strikeouts in 112 2/3 innings and was named the Southland Conference’s Pitcher of the Year. The Lions reached the Tuscaloosa Regional with her help.

“I absolutely loved my two years at Southeastern. It was a dream come true,” Burns said. “I met some of the best friends, and I had the best experiences through softball, relationships with professors, the school. That university was so good to me, and I had a great time there.”

Southeastern Louisiana's coach, Rick Fremin, left to be an assistant coach at Texas after this past season, which prompted Burns to mull over her options. Through Bible studies and more, she thought it was time to move on and that Baylor was the place to go.

“I know it seems crazy to everybody,” she said. “It's farther from home. It doesn't make, I guess, the most sense when you think about it that way. But whenever I got there, I just knew that it was going to be a good place for me to take those next steps.”

Burns won’t soon forget what both Ole Miss and Southeastern Louisiana did for her as a player and a person, but she’s eager to see what Baylor can do for her as well.

“It's nice to be able to have those aspects from different schools, and also add on fresh eyes,” she said. “And hopefully have a lot of different tools in your belt to use at this level of competition.”

title: Kareem Abdul-Jabbar Shares Strong Message About Caitlin Clark
description: Indiana Fever superstar Caitlin Clark is in her third WNBA season, and many believe she’s already become the face of the league. However, the physical play opponents have used against her has sparked plenty of debate throughout the season.In a recent Substack post, NBA legend and college basketball icon Kareem Abdul-Jabbar addressed that discussion.He argued […] The post Kareem Abdul-Jabbar Shares Strong Message About Caitlin Clark appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
Indiana Fever superstar Caitlin Clark is in her third WNBA season, and many believe she’s already become the face of the league. However, the physical play opponents have used against her has sparked plenty of debate throughout the season.

In a recent Substack post, NBA legend and college basketball icon Kareem Abdul-Jabbar addressed that discussion.

He argued that calling Clark “the face of the league” — the phrase Congressman August Pfluger used in a recent letter to WNBA Commissioner Cathy Engelbert about Clark’s treatment — is “disrespectful” to the league’s other stars.

Abdul-Jabbar praised Clark’s talent but said it’s too early to give her that label.

“My first reaction to this letter was to check the calendar and make sure it wasn’t April Fools’ Day,” Abdul-Jabbar wrote. “Don’t get me wrong: Clark is a very good, possibly even a great, player. But calling any one player the face of the league, absent the sort of on-court and cross-platform dominance of a Michael Jordan or a LeBron James, is an insult to an awful lot of great players.”

Kareem specifically pointed to players such as A’ja Wilson and Breanna Stewart as stars who deserve consideration for that title based on what they’ve accomplished throughout their professional careers.

🏀 Kareem Abdul-Jabbar isn’t questioning Caitlin Clark’s talent or impact. He’s questioning whether one young star should already be labeled “the face of the WNBA” while accomplished veterans such as A’ja Wilson, Breanna Stewart, Napheesa Collier, Chelsea Gray and Alyssa Thomas… pic.twitter.com/GUu1MpnHCS

— Wayne DuPree (@RealWayneDupree) July 15, 2026

Whether Clark deserves to be the face of the league remains open for debate. That said, there’s little question she has helped raise the WNBA’s profile since the Indiana Fever selected her No. 1 overall out of Iowa in 2024.

During that span, the league has added expansion teams, increased the regular-season schedule, and set records for fan engagement. Attendance, television viewership, and social media interest have all surged.

The WNBA also reached a new collective bargaining agreement before the season that significantly increased player salaries.

Clark isn’t solely responsible for the league’s recent growth. Still, she has been one of its biggest driving forces without question.

Like HEAVY's content? Be sure to follow us.

This article was originally published on HEAVY

The post Kareem Abdul-Jabbar Shares Strong Message About Caitlin Clark appeared first on HEAVY.

title: College Softball Player Gabriella Muñoz Dies at 19: ‘Completely Heartbroken’
description: Muñoz competed in 27 games for the Livingstone College Blue Bears last season
fullText:
Livingstone College announced the death of 19-year-old softball player Gabriella Muñoz

Muñoz, who played 27 games for the Blue Bears last season, reportedly died while in Texas, the school said

A cause of death has not been made known and the school asked “that inaccurate information and speculation cease”

Livingstone College is mourning the loss of 19-year-old softball player Gabriella Muñoz.

The North Carolina school announced her death in a statement on Friday, July 10, with her coach calling the loss “completely heartbreaking.”

“It is with profound sadness and heavy hearts that Livingstone College shares the passing of our beloved student-athlete, Gabriella Muñoz, a rising junior from Katy, Texas, and a cherished member of the Blue Bear softball family,” Livingstone College said in a statement.

“Gabriella represented the very best of Livingstone College. As No. 55 on the Blue Bear softball team, she embraced her role as a versatile utility player with determination, resilience, and an unwavering team-first mentality. While her athletic abilities made her a valuable competitor, it was her bubbly personality, gentle spirit, and genuine kindness that truly distinguished her” the school added.

Muñoz reportedly died while she was in Texas, the college said, while calling out “false” reports that claimed she died while at the school.

“Reports claiming that Livingstone College student-athlete Gabriella Muñoz passed away on campus are false. Gabriella passed away while in Texas, away from campus. Out of respect for her family and our campus community, we ask that inaccurate information and speculation cease,” the school said.

A cause of death has not been made known. An obituary for Muñoz said she “left this world on July 9, 2026, just shy of her 20th birthday.”

“Gabi was born on July 26, 2006, in Katy, Texas to loving parents Brandon and Jennifer Muñoz. Her bright spirit and infectious smile touched the lives of everyone she met,” the obituary said.

Follow your favorite athletes on and off the field with PEOPLE’s free sports newsletter — 

Muñoz competed in 27 games and started one for the Blue Bears last season, according to Syracuse.com.

Livingstone College’s head softball coach Steve Wallace said in a statement: “I am completely heartbroken by the passing of Gabriella. She was a complete joy to have as a part of the program. A great athlete and an even better student. The program will miss her immensely and her absence will be felt.”

Livingstone will make “support resources” available to students, faculty and staff, the school said.

“Although Gabriella’s time with us was far too short, her impact will endure. She leaves behind a legacy of academic excellence, athletic determination, unwavering kindness, and Blue Bear pride that will continue to inspire our community for years to come,” the school’s statement continued, adding, “On behalf of Livingstone College and the entire Blue Bear family, we honor Gabriella’s life with gratitude and love. She will forever remain a cherished member of our Livingstone family.”

title: Where is former LSU quarterback Max Johnson playing this year?
description: Former LSU football QB Max Johnson has spent a long time in college football. Johnson will play for his fourth team this fall.
fullText:
Former LSU football quarterback Max Johnson started his college football career at LSU in 2020. Since then, he transferred two times and retained years of eligibility due to multiple injury stints. He holds a seventh year of eligibility and plans to use it in 2026.

Johnson will play for Georgia Southern this fall. It is his fourth school in seven years. Last year was his second at North Carolina. The passer played under head coach Bill Belichick, appearing in four games and completing 62.1% of his passes for 432 yards. He played his first year for the Tar Heels in 2024 but suffered a season-ending injury in the season opener.

LSU recruited Johnson as a consensus four-star prospect out of Watkinsville, GA. He made six appearances in 2020 before taking over as the full-time starter in 2021 when he threw for 2,815 yards and 27 touchdowns. He as recruited by head coach Ed Orgeron but decided to move on after Orgeron departed Baton Rouge.

Max Johnson started for LSU the year after Joe Burrow won the Heisman… he’s still in College Football

The last of the 7 year college players 🫡 pic.twitter.com/iiH8Vp69MP

— SleeperCFB (@SleeperCFB) July 13, 2026

Johnson took his talents to Texas A&M in 2022 for two years. He redshirted after suffering a season-ending injury in the fourth game of the year. The following year, he stepped into the top role when the regular starter got hurt. He totaled 1,452 passing yards and nine touchdowns through the air for the Aggies then moved to North Carolina.

title: College football analyst says ACC has new face of the conference
description: College football season is around the corner. In a little over a month, a new season will kick off, and the quest for the College Football Playoff will begin. ESPN’s FPI has already laid out the favorites for 2026. The…
fullText:
College football season is around the corner. In a little over a month, a new season will kick off, and the quest for the College Football Playoff will begin. ESPN’s FPI has already laid out the favorites for 2026.

The 2026 college football ESPN FPI includes a familiar face in the top 10 , though perhaps the ACC now has a new face leading the way. With Clemson’s window of dominance closing, the 2025 runners-up might be taking that spot.

MORE: College football analyst has strong dark horse for the College Football Playoffs

Prominent college football analyst Paul Finebaum declared that the Miami Hurricanes are now the face of the ACC. He joined McElroy and Cubelic in the Morning and explained why Miami has dethroned the Clemson Tigers.

“I think this is the first time in a long time that Miami takes center stage,” Finebaum said. “They played for the title. They almost won it. They are now the face of the league.”

That’s an understandable argument. Miami probably would have won it all if not for Curt Cignetti and Indiana’s run of the century. Mario Cristobal endured two rough seasons to begin his tenure in South Beach.

MORE: Brett Yormark’s silence on Brendan Sorsby draws sharp criticism from Paul Finebaum

However, 2024 showed the Hurricanes looking like a familiar Miami team again. They won 10 regular-season games and the Florida Cup. The school then landed Carson Beck from Georgia in the transfer portal, and he became a crucial part of their run alongside Malachi Toney.

Miami snuck into the College Football Playoff despite not winning the ACC. They went on a massive run that included a road win at Texas A&M, then dethroned defending champion Ohio State. Finally, they defeated a resurgent Ole Miss team to punch their ticket to the national title game.

If Miami wants to become the face of the ACC, though, the Hurricanes can’t afford a down season. They also need to win a conference title this year. After landing Darian Mensah in the transfer portal, they have a more than legitimate shot to win it all.

There’s no denying Miami is on the rise. But have they really become the face of the ACC?

title: Coming home: Howard transfers to Shawnee State from Glenville State
description: When a program is making the transition to a new landscape of college athletics, there are plenty of challenges that come with that. However, those challenges are certainly made easier when a team gets a player who competed at the NCAA Division II College World Series. And they're made even easier when said player understands the passion and pride that it takes to win in said area. AndiJo ...
fullText:
When a program is making the transition to a new landscape of college athletics, there are plenty of challenges that come with that.

However, those challenges are certainly made easier when a team gets a player who competed at the NCAA Division II College World Series.

And they're made even easier when said player understands the passion and pride that it takes to win in said area.

AndiJo Howard, who led Wheelersburg to back-to-back OHSAA Division III State Championships in the sport of softball in 2022 and 2023 as the team's ace pitcher and helped lead Glenville State to 51 wins and the NCAA Division II College World Series as a sophomore this past season, will transfer to Shawnee State this coming academic year in a monstrous offseason pickup.

She will have no less than two seasons left to play for the Bears, who add a key pitching rotation and potential designated player piece that could prove to be quite vital as Shawnee State begins its NCAA Division II transition this coming academic year.

Howard, who will reunite with fellow Little League World Series and Wheelersburg High School teammates and champions Haley Myers and Sydney Skiver, is ready to help Shawnee State attack what will be a new era.

"It means everything to me to get to come home and finish my career," Howard said. "I am a firm believer that God puts you in places at the right time for the right reasons. It just feels right to come back home and give back to my community. I can’t wait to see familiar faces at every game and play with some of my best friends again. I’ve played with some of these girls since we were six, so it’s very full circle. I also can’t wait to just get to be near my family. They mean so much to me, especially my Mammaw Janet, my parents and siblings, and my aunt Beth."

Howard, who began playing the sport of softball at four years of age, started getting into the sport seriously as she began attacking travel ball, notably with Buckeye Elite, where her best friend, Macee Eaton, and others also played.

As sixth graders, both Howard and Eaton made the transition to playing with the Ohio Hawks. At that point, matters became more serious for both players as each dreamt of playing at the collegiate level, with both players fueling each other's fire.

"I really started of thinking about the next level pretty early. I started playing local travel ball on Buckeye Elite and have so many fun memories from that. Jason Smith being our coach made the tournaments so fun, but also competitive. Around the sixth grade, I started playing on the Ohio Hawks. Macee Eaton and I started playing on the team together and that’s when things started to become more serious. It’s been such a fun experience getting to grow and learn the game with her. Everyone around here knows she’s such an incredible athlete, but she's an even better human being who I’m blessed to call my best friend. During the years of travel ball, I learned so much and grew so much as a player, I quickly knew I wanted to play at the next level."

Howard also had deep roots in the sport of softball. Part of a set of sisters who were basically raised in the game, the Howards' father, Andy, played and continues to play a big role in the sport at Wheelersburg, particularly with the Wheelersburg Little League.

An older sister, Faith, was the ace pitcher on Wheelersburg softball's first state championship team, and went on to pitch for NCAA Division I Northern Kentucky. Later on, Faith got an opportunity to work with the Virginia Tech softball staff as a manager.

Layna, a younger sister to AndiJo, was Wheelersburg's starting second baseman on this past season's state championship team and looks to have her own promising future in the offing.

"Everyone in the Wheelersburg community is just so encouraging and supportive," Howard said. "The coaching staff at Wheelersburg goes above and beyond for their players. Wheelersburg is just like no other. I was lucky to grow up around the ball fields from such a young age watching my sister Faith, that I knew how the program was run. (Teresa) Ruby, (Susan) Reutzel, Dusty (Salyers), Jackie (Hawk-Conley), they’re all in it to win, and that’s all you could want as a player. The memories on and off the field with everyone are times that I’ll always remember. I remember them more from the amazing people they are."

AndiJo's own potential in the sport was showcased to the state, country and world during one magical summer in 2018.

Having already thrown a complete game one-hitter in Wheelersburg's district championship victory over Northwest, Howard added in two complete games against Tallmadge and Canfield at the state level, posting 19 strikeouts while only giving up four hits in her two starts en route to leading the Wheelersburg 11-12 All-Stars to the state championship.

Similar dominance in the regional tournament followed, where Howard threw a no-hitter with 12 strikeouts to begin Central Region play against Indiana and then added in a complete game two-hitter with 13 strikeouts and just one walk in a 3-1 win over Iowa that sent the Wheelersburg 11-12s to the 2018 Little League Softball World Series.

In 33 innings at the World Series, Howard allowed just one earned run. She threw five complete games, including a complete game two-hit shutout with 10 strikeouts against Mexico, a three-inning relief performance with eight strikeouts and no hits allowed in a come-from-behind win over the Philippines, and then, over the final three days of the tournament, a complete game two-hitter with 12 strikeouts against Washington, a complete game six-hitter with five strikeouts against Louisiana, and the title game performance, a complete game three-hit shutout with seven strikeouts against Pennsylvania.

The Wheelersburg 11-12 Softball All-Stars, and Howard, had become names known to many across the country and world nearly overnight.

"The World Series was definitely one of the best summers of my life. We basically lived at the ball fields," Howard said. "I loved every single second of it. It’s amazing to see where everyone on that team has ended up. Everyone has grown into not only amazing ball players but also amazing people. Most of us play at different places now but I know those are my friends for life. I cannot wait to get to play with Sydney (Skiver) and Haley (Myers) again. They are such competitors and are so fun to play with. I have to give big credit to Dusty (Salyers) for how he coached us from such a young age. He had us doing things right from the very start, and he’s influenced our lives in so many ways. It’s amazing to see where everyone on the team is in their lives right now, everyone’s doing amazing things."

But it was a precursor of things to come.

In her freshman season, Howard didn't just help Wheelersburg get back to the OHSAA State Final Four for the first time in four years — she helped the Lady Pirates compete at that level.

As a freshman, Howard was Wheelersburg's ace pitcher and threw all seven innings against eventual state champion Sherwood Fairview, all while going 1-for-2 with two walks from the No. 6 spot in the batting order.

Wheelersburg finished Howard's freshman year 26-2 overall.

The next two years would see Howard — and the entire Wheelersburg softball program — take matters up a notch.

In 2022, Howard, hitting in the middle of the order as the team's cleanup hitter and again serving as its ace pitcher, threw a complete game four-hitter with five strikeouts and no walks on 91 pitches in her return to the OHSAA State Semifinals, leading Wheelersburg to a 5-2 win over Cardington-Lincoln at that point.

The following day saw Howard spin an efficient five inning performance where Howard threw just 49 pitches, before giving way to freshman talent Kaylynn Carter who finished matters off with the save in a 5-2 state championship victory over Massillon Tuslaw. Howard also went 2-for-2 at the plate, driving in two of the team's five runs offensively to boot.

Howard now had a state title of her own in the trophy case in addition to the World Series title. Wheelersburg finished 27-1 in 2022.

In a rather dominant display, the Lady Pirates went undefeated, wire-to-wire, going 32-0 behind what would be an Honorable Mention All-Ohio campaign for the third-year ace. Howard threw five-and-two-thirds innings of seven-hit softball in a 6-1 victory for Wheelersburg over Canfield South Range in the 2023 OHSAA State Semifinals, then proceeded to follow that state semifinal effort with an elite performance in the state title game against Lewistown Indian Lake, throwing a complete game two-hit shutout on just 64 pitches in Wheelersburg's 6-0 victory.

Wheelersburg had gone back-to-back and repeated in doing so with Howard as the team's ace pitcher once again.

Howard then earned First-Team All-Ohio honors as a senior to complete her career in a Wheelersburg uniform. She helped lead the Lady Pirates to a 106-6 overall record in her four seasons playing under Teresa Ruby, while serving as the ace of the pitching staff across all four seasons of her coaching career.

"Playing at Wheelersburg was such a blessing," Howard said. "There are so many people to give credit to that have impacted my life so much. I’m forever thankful for the coaches there and what they did to impact me. They really did a good job preparing us for the next level and what’s to come with that. Teresa, (Susan) Reutzel, Dusty, Jackie, and Mike (Estep), they’re all just amazing people. I’m forever grateful for all I’ve learned from them. They allowed us to compete and always put together a competitive schedule to be successful during post season and ultimately set us up for success in college. So many of the girls went on to play at the next level and it’s super exciting to see how successful each of them are. Playing against Emma, and watching Catie and Macee on TV, it’s just so incredible."

Throughout her first two seasons at Glenville State, Howard helped the Pioneers go 87-35 overall and 46-14 in the Mountain East Conference. In her freshman season, Howard went 7-2 in her 17 pitching appearances and nine starts. She posted five complete games and nabbed an ERA of 2.60, all while hitting .304 at the plate as well. Glenville State went 36-17.

This past season, Howard played a larger role offensively, playing in 57 games and starting in 25 of those contests. Her leadership was critical in the Pioneers improving to 51-18 and winning not only the Mountain East Conference Tournament Championship, but the NCAA Division II Atlantic Regional and Super Regional Championships as well en route to the program's first-ever NCAA Division II World Series appearance. There, Glenville State took No. 2 Missouri Southern State and No. 3 North Georgia into competitive outings in the latter innings before dropping 3-2 and 5-0 decisions.

But in addition to her goals on the softball diamond, Howard's goals off of the diamond have always been at the forefront. Shawnee State's occupational therapy program was always attractive to Howard, and between that, the Bears' impending move to NCAA Division II competition in the Mountain East, and playing closer to her family, all of those reasons played a factor in her final decision.

"Shawnee State attracted me for several reasons," Howard said. "I’ve had a goal of being an occupational therapist for a few years now and Shawnee State has one of the top OT programs around so that definitely was a huge factor into it. With this, they’re building a new field and I know the coaches there have done a great job turning the program around. I felt confident staying in the MEC and I love the competitive culture that it brings. I am very thankful for my two years at Glenville State, I had the opportunity of playing at the D2 World Series which is something you always dream of as a little girl. I met some of my very best friends who I will be friends with for the rest of my life. Transferring was a big decision because I loved the girls so much. With Glenville being in the same conference as Shawnee, I’m honestly just excited to keep competing in the MEC and hopefully getting Shawnee State its first MEC Championship. I’m hoping to lead by example and to help bring them their first D2 championship in softball. Knowing what it takes now, I plan on leading them to an MEC Championship."

She's certainly excited with the plans that she's seeing take shape at Shawnee State.

"I’m so excited that Shawnee is bringing in so many new additions," Howard said. "I think it’s such a rewarding experience for Scioto County. So many people pour into so many events and activities and I truly think Shawnee deserves to have new things. I’m super thankful that this is happening when I am returning home."

In addition to having the opportunity to win a Mountain East Conference title with two different softball programs, Howard will also get to be closer to her younger sister, who is leaving her own mark as a dependable infielder for the Lady Pirates.

Perhaps in fitting fashion considering the family's lineage to Wheelersburg softball, it was Layna Howard who recorded the last defensive assist in Wheelersburg's latest state championship victory, a 7-6 triumph over Baltimore Liberty Union where the Lady Pirates rallied from a 4-1 deficit to claim the victory.

"Winning a championship is always special," Howard said. "Getting to do something like that twice was really incredible. Playing for Wheelersburg softball is such a special experience. Everyone is in it for the one goal and that goal is to win a state championship. It’s incredible to look at how much softball has grown in our area and how it continues to grow. I love that people view Wheelersburg as a power house in softball. Getting to do that with my best friends is something I’ll remember forever. Now my younger sister Layna has accomplished the same goal and I think that it’s so cool that my family has gotten to experience this so many times. Layna is such a hard worker and an amazing human that it’s super cool that she got to experience a back to back championship as well."

Howard, who has a goal of getting SSU two MEC Softball Championships alongside two of her best friends, Myers and Skiver, and for the community that she loves before moving on to obtain her master's degree in Shawnee State's three-plus-two occupational therapy program and becoming an occupational therapist following her softball career, says embracing each opportunity that lies ahead.

It's worked pretty hard for the former World Series Champion and two-time OHSAA State Softball Champion, because Howard, who is rarely seen without a smile or an optimistic mindset, has the mentality of an optimist.

"My advice is to dream big, work hard, and always believe that God’s going to put you in the place you go for a reason," Howard said. "Whether it’s the place or the people, each experience you receive is a blessing. Play with Joy, be a good teammate, and enjoy the experience. Every chance we get is a blessing from God and whether you win a championship each year or not, knowing we have something so much greater to live for is something so important to remember when times get hard on the field."

title: Ranking Florida's top fantasy football options entering 2026 season
description: Pinpointing which Florida Gators you should be watching as your prepare for your college fantasy football draft this fall.
fullText:
Yahoo Sports' new college fantasy football platform allows followers of the sport to draft players from their favorite programs. With a revamped roster, featuring more than 50 additions, the Gators may have more uncertainty than established star power for owners to consider. However, there are a number of surefire producers to target in the draft's initial rounds.

Star running back Jadan Baugh is the clear No. 1 option for the Gators under new offensive coordinator Buster Faulkner. The returning 1,000-yard rusher is likely to sustain his productive level from 2025 as the junior becomes more of a focal point for the Orange and Blue. Inexperience at the quarterback position makes Baugh the centerpiece for Jon Sumrall and company in 2026. The All-SEC playmaker will see an increased workload with plenty of red zone opportunities. Baugh projects as a potential high-end fantasy starter.

Behind Baugh is last year's breakout freshman Vernell Brown III. He led Florida in receiving yards last season in his first year with the Gators, making history by becoming the first true freshman in program history to earn All-SEC honors at three different positions in a single season. Brown enters 2026 as one of the team's most important players; the Gainesville, Florida, native's 773 all-purpose yards ranked second among all true freshman wide receivers, highlighting his versatility and impact — a big reason many feel he'll continue improving.

Auburn transfer Eric Singleton Jr. provides a more experienced option and is the only receiver on Florida's roster who has eclipsed 600 yards in a single season. Dallas Wilson also deserves attention after showing flashes of elite potential during an injury-shortened freshman season.

Florida's eventual starting quarterback will no doubt have fantasy relevance, but managers should be wary of both Aaron Philo and Tramell Jones Jr., treating the pair as valuable backup options.

Jadan Baugh, RB - Potential top-20 running back

Vernell Brown III, WR - Potential top-50 receiver

Eric Singleton Jr., WR - Potential top-50 receiver

Aaron Philo or Tramell Jones Jr., QB - QB2

Dallas Wilson, WR - Late-round sleeper or waiver wire target

title: Man who killed Dartmouth professors at 17 to get a chance at parole in about 20 years, judge rules
description: A Vermont man who was 17 when he and a friend killed a pair of married Dartmouth College professors 25 years ago will have a chance at parole in about 20 years, when he reaches the age of one of his v...
fullText:
CONCORD, N.H. (AP) — A Vermont man who was 17 when he and a friend killed a pair of married Dartmouth College professors 25 years ago will have a chance at parole in about 20 years, when he reaches the age of one of his victims, a judge ruled Monday.

Lawyers for Robert Tulloch, now 43, and prosecutors reached an agreement, avoiding a three-day planned resentencing hearing. In court Monday, a shackled Tulloch held his head down and appeared to breathe heavily as the horrific details of the stabbings were recounted.

Tulloch was automatically sentenced to life without parole after pleading guilty to first-degree murder in the 2001 stabbing deaths of Half and Susanne Zantop. But the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in 2012 that mandatory sentences of life without parole are unconstitutional for juveniles, and later applied that decision retroactively.

The rulings gave hundreds of juvenile lifers a shot at freedom, including five men serving life sentences in New Hampshire for murders they committed as teenagers. Tulloch's resentencing hearing, the last of the five, would have begun Monday in Grafton County Superior Court in North Haverhill, New Hampshire.

Tulloch apologized to one of the professors' two daughters, Veronika Zantop, who joined the hearing remotely, and talked about how she and her family were affected by the death of her parents.

A psychiatrist with two sons, one of them the same age Tulloch was when he committed his crimes, she said she can appreciate that brain functioning can change over time. But she does not believe it's true for Tulloch, saying he meticulously planned the killings and followed through in a cold, predatory manner.

"This wasn't a crime of passion or retribution," she said. "He wasn't using substances, he wasn't psychotic. There was just sheer depravity." She urged that he stay in prison "for the longest possible sentence."

Tulloch abandoned his prepared statement.

"After listening to that, I feel disgusted by even thinking I could say anything that would mean anything," he said.

In a court filing last week, Tulloch's lawyers argued that a minimum sentence in the range of 30 to 40 years is appropriate, based on a review of other murders committed by juveniles in New Hampshire and cases nationwide that were affected by the Supreme Court rulings.

Judge Lawrence MacLeod resentenced Tulloch to a minimum of 45 years to life. He could be considered for parole in 2046 when he's 62 years old, the same age Half Zantop was when he was killed.

MacLeod said he reviewed the applicable law, the circumstances of Tulloch's offenses, his conduct while in prison, the outcomes of the other New Hampshire cases and Veronika Zantop's statement.

"The agreed upon sentence provides certainty that Tulloch will remain incarcerated for a substantial period of time, allows Tulloch to pursue some measure of rehabilitation, and it secures important protections for the community," New Hampshire Attorney General John Formella said in a statement.

Attorneys Richard Guerriero and Oliver Bloom said Tulloch's prison records show he has matured, and that after some initial misconduct early on, he's had no major infractions since 2012 and no minor infractions since 2017.

Quoting from Tulloch's therapy records, they said he has expressed "significant remorse" for what he sees as a heinous and unforgivable crime, his "warped youthful thinking," and his "good capacity for empathy."

According to Tulloch's friend, James Parker, the teens were bored with their lives in Chelsea, Vermont, when they concocted a plan to kill strangers, steal their money and move to Australia. For several months, they knocked on doors in New Hampshire and Vermont pretending to be conducting a survey on the environment before being let in by the Zantops. Susanne Zantop, 55, was head of Dartmouth's German studies department and her husband, Half Zantop taught Earth sciences.

Parker, who was 16 at the time, told prosecutors Tulloch stabbed Half Zantop and then directed Parker to attack Susanne Zantop. Tulloch also stabbed her. Fingerprints on a knife sheath and a bloody boot print linked the teens to the crime, but after being questioned by police, they fled Vermont and hitchhiked west. They were arrested at an Indiana truck stop weeks later.

Parker, who cooperated with prosecutors and pleaded guilty to being an accomplice to second-degree murder, was released from prison on parole in 2024 at age 40, having served nearly the minimum term of his 25-years-to-life sentence.

"I think it's unimaginably horrible," Parker said during his parole hearing when asked by a board member what he thought of what he did. "I know there's not an amount of time or things that I can do to change it, or alleviate any pain that I've caused."

The Supreme Court rulings addressed only mandatory life sentences without parole for juveniles, leaving the U.S. the only country that allows discretionary life sentences for minors. Twenty-eight states and the District of Columbia have banned the practice, while another five states allow it but have no one serving such a sentence, according to the Campaign for the Fair Sentencing of Youth.

New Hampshire lawmakers have rejected attempts to end life sentences for juveniles, but Tulloch's case could bolster future attempts. After Tulloch argued in 2018 that sentencing juveniles to life without parole violated the state constitution, the judge asked the state Supreme Court to weigh in, but it declined. Last July, MacLeod agreed with Tulloch, finding that the constitution categorically prohibits such sentences as "cruel or unusual" punishment.

Among the juvenile lifers nationwide who have been resentenced after the U.S. Supreme Court rulings, more than 75% have received sentences of less than 40 years, according to a study published in 2024 in the Journal of Criminal Justice.

In New Hampshire, one man was resentenced to life without parole after refusing to attend his hearing or authorize his attorneys to argue for a lesser sentence. Others received sentences of 25-, 40- and 45-years-to-life.

title: ‘He’s Very Stubborn’ — College Football Insider Questions Dabo Swinney’s High-Stakes Clemson Gamble
description: Clemson coach Dabo Swinney finds himself among the coaches facing intense national pressure heading into next season after a disappointing finish in 2025. Immediately after the Tigers’ Pinstripe Bowl loss to the Penn State Nittany Lions, Swinney revamped his staff by firing offensive coordinator Garrett Riley and hiring Chad Morris.
fullText:
Clemson coach Dabo Swinney finds himself among the coaches facing intense national pressure heading into next season after a disappointing finish in 2025. Immediately after the Tigers’ Pinstripe Bowl loss to the Penn State Nittany Lions, Swinney revamped his staff by firing offensive coordinator Garrett Riley and hiring Chad Morris.

Swinney has been very active in the transfer portal as well during the offseason, but after losing Cade Klubnik to the 2026 NFL Draft, his quarterback room is at its weakest and having not improved is a big gamble, with the season ultimately depending on how the offense holds up.

Swinney has found himself in the crosshairs of fans and analysts alike for his reluctance to embrace the transfer portal. However, during the winter transfer window, the Clemson coach bucked that trend by signing roughly 10 transfers, mostly on defense to help rejuvenate his roster.

Most of those additions, though, came on the defensive side of the ball, while the offense appears to be trending in the opposite direction after averaging just 27.2 points per game last season, a significant drop from the 34.7 points it averaged the year before.

Swinney lost quarterback Cade Klubnik and OL Blake Miller to the NFL Draft. He now enters the season with four linemen who either lack experience or are returning from season-ending injuries, and despite that uncertainty, he chose not to add a quarterback through the portal, even though the five quarterbacks currently on Clemson’s roster have combined for just one career start.

BE THE GM OF YOUR FAVORITE TEAM: PFN’s FREE NFL Mock Draft Simulator

That’s certainly a big gamble, and college football insider Chris Hummer isn’t a fan of the decision.

“Well, I’m fascinated by Clemson,” he told CBS Sports. “In some ways, Dabo Swinney joined the modern era. They had double-digit transfers this year for the first time. They totally reset on the defensive side of the ball. Dabo had the press conference of the year calling out Pete Golding and Ole Miss. Like, it was classic Dabo.

“But like the question I have for Clemson and the thing I’m most curious about is like, what does their quarterback situation look like, and why didn’t they go get a guy in the portal? I’m very curious how Dabo talks about that room because I think there’s a ton of pressure on him and he’s very stubborn. So, we’ll see how that goes.”

Swinney has yet to settle on a QB1, but after spring practice, the competition has narrowed to redshirt junior Christopher Vizzina and freshman Tait Reynolds, with Vizzina currently leading the race.

REDRAFT THE ENTIRE NFL: PFN’s FREE NFL Ultimate Redraft Simulator

Vizzina redshirted his first season at Clemson in 2023 and has appeared in 14 games for the Tigers, including one start. He has completed 64 of 105 passes for 596 yards, 4 touchdowns, and 1 interception.

Reynolds, meanwhile, is a four-star prospect and the 11th-best quarterback in the class of 2026, per ESPN. He chose Clemson over football offers from Florida State, Miami, Oklahoma and Texas A&M, and had previously committed to Arizona State for baseball.

While the competition remains open, Vizzina has enormous shoes to fill after Klubnik posted a PFSN College QB Impact score of 79.2 with 16 passing touchdowns last season, and with Reynolds not far behind him, he could quickly find himself under pressure.

And if both don’t work with the Tigers’ already weak offensive line, Swinney will have a lot to answer for at Clemson.

title: Ohio State hoops to play in doubleheader with other Ohio teams
description: For the first time ever, Ohio's four major college basketball teams will take part in an event together in a preseason exhibition outdoors.
fullText:
Well, what do we have here? Based on multiple reports, the cows have come home, hell has frozen over, and pigs have flown. That's because somewhere, somehow, the four most historic Ohio basketball programs are getting together to play an event on the same court. That's right. Ohio State, Cincinnati, Xavier, and Dayton are all set to play in the CareSource Invitational, an exhibition event to raise awareness and funds for mental health and suicide prevention.

So which of the other three teams will the Buckeyes play? They will be matched up with Cincinnati, while the other game will pit Dayton vs. Xavier. To add a little intrigue, the game will take place outdoors at the Lindner Family Tennis Center, home of the Cincinnati Open, on October 7, very early on in the preseason. If the weather becomes an issue, the event will be moved to UD Arena in Dayton. However, the preference is clearly to have this thing be an outdoor event to add to the atmosphere.

“I think this is great. This is great for the basketball community in Ohio. And it’s been a long time coming,” Ohio State head coach Jake Diebler said on Thursday. “I’ve been vocal about this since I first got the job and wanted something like this to happen. There are so many moving parts. There are so many moving parts to make something like this happen. It takes a lot of people. And I’m just excited. Everyone got together to make it happen.”

Buckeyes 🆚 Bearcats at the Lindner Family Tennis Center 🏟️

📝 https://t.co/TtPzSYSaml#GoBucks | #FightToTheEnd pic.twitter.com/8cD4l8V64p

— Ohio State Hoops (@OhioStateHoops) July 9, 2026

We've been calling for an annual event like this for a while here on Buckeyes Wire, and we'll have more on that later, but for now, the only way you can see the CareSource Invitational is to purchase tickets because it will not be televised. When information on how to get your hands on some tickets becomes available, we'll bring that information to you.
